lofi.name: Lo-Fi Hip Hop
lofi.description: Mellow beats built on imperfection — vinyl crackle, tape hiss, detuned jazz piano. Rooted in underground hip-hop sampling culture. Designed to be heard in the background: studying, coding, reading, winding down. The aesthetic intentionally degrades audio quality to create warmth.
lofi.production_notes: Analog saturation, pitch wobble, and room reverb are characteristic. Deliberately low-fidelity mastering. BPM rarely exceeds 90. Vocals, when present, are pitched-down samples rather than original performances.

pop.name: Pop
pop.description: Mainstream-accessible music built for broad appeal. Verse-chorus structure, polished production, memorable hooks. Contemporary pop blends elements from R&B, EDM, and hip-hop into a radio-ready package. Maximizes catchiness per minute.
pop.production_notes: Heavy bus compression, sidechain pumping on synth pads, layered vocal harmonies. Vocal production is clean and upfront. Reverb and delay used for spatial width. Mastered loud for streaming.

rock.name: Rock
rock.description: Guitar-driven music emphasizing live performance energy. Ranges from classic rock anthems to alternative and indie rock. Defined by electric guitar tones, rhythm section interplay, and dynamic contrast between quiet verses and loud choruses.
rock.production_notes: Guitar tones range from clean jangle to heavy distortion. Drums are often recorded live with natural room sound. Mix emphasizes punchy low-mids. Compression is moderate — rock preserves more dynamic range than pop.

jazz.name: Jazz
jazz.description: Improvisational music rooted in blues and swing traditions. Defined by complex harmonies, syncopated rhythms, and real-time musical conversation between instruments. From bebop to cool jazz to bossa nova, jazz is the genre most defined by process rather than product.
jazz.production_notes: Typically recorded live or semi-live to preserve ensemble dynamics. Minimal compression to maintain the natural attack of acoustic instruments. Piano, upright bass, and brush kit are foundational. Warm room sound is preferred over dry recording.

ambient.name: Ambient
ambient.description: Textural, atmospheric music designed to shape the listening environment rather than demand attention. Pioneered by Brian Eno in the late 1970s. Emphasizes tone and texture over melody and rhythm. Long evolving pads, minimal percussion, and a sense of endless space.
ambient.production_notes: Often processed through hardware or software reverb chains with decay times of 10+ seconds. Heavy use of delay, pitch-shifting, and time-stretching. Rhythm is implied or completely absent. Dynamic changes are glacially slow.

synthwave.name: Synthwave
synthwave.description: Retro-futuristic electronic music drawing from 1980s film scores, video game soundtracks, and synth-pop. Characterized by analog synthesizer pads, gated reverb on snare drums, arpeggiated basslines, and a nostalgic aesthetic of neon lights and late-night highways.
synthwave.production_notes: Vintage synthesizers (Juno-106, Jupiter-8, DX7) are preferred. Gated reverb on drums is the most recognizable sonic signature. Tempo sits in the 90-120 BPM range — fast enough to drive but slow enough to feel contemplative. Mixing favors a wide stereo field.

hip-hop.name: Hip-Hop
hip-hop.description: Rhythmic vocal music built on sampled or produced beats. From boom-bap to trap, hip-hop spans an enormous range of tempos and textures. Speechiness — the density of rhythmic spoken word — is the defining acoustic marker that separates it from other genres.
hip-hop.production_notes: 808 bass is foundational in modern trap sub-genres. Boom-bap relies on chopped vinyl samples. Vocal delivery (flow) and lyrical content are as important as the instrumental. Modern trap uses triplet hi-hat patterns at 130-160 BPM, often with intentionally slow rap cadences.

classical.name: Classical
classical.description: Western art music spanning baroque counterpoint through romantic orchestration to 20th-century modernism. Emphasizes compositional structure, acoustic instrument timbres, and emotional depth through dynamic range. The genre with the highest acousticness and instrumentalness scores — no electronic production.
classical.production_notes: Minimal processing — classical recordings aim for acoustic transparency. Hall reverb captures the natural room. Dynamic range is intentionally preserved (no limiting). Piano, strings, and orchestra are the primary timbres.

metal.name: Metal
metal.description: Extreme guitar-driven music maximizing intensity, distortion, and technical complexity. From classic heavy metal to death metal and progressive metal. Defined by heavily distorted down-tuned guitars, blast-beat drum patterns, and an overall density of sound that pushes the limits of acoustic dynamics.
metal.production_notes: Guitar tones use heavy saturation through high-gain amplifiers. Double bass drum is standard. Mix is extremely compressed — wall-of-sound density. Tempo is the highest in the catalog at 140-200+ BPM. Modern production uses parallel compression on drums.

r&b.name: R&B (Rhythm and Blues)
r&b.description: Soulful, groove-oriented music blending African American blues traditions with modern production. Contemporary R&B features lush vocal harmonies, smooth syncopated basslines, and intimate lyrical themes about love and relationships. Bridges the gap between pop accessibility and soul depth.
r&b.production_notes: Vocals recorded close and warm — intimate, breathy delivery is prized. Reverb is tasteful and not excessive. Groove comes from syncopated bass and drums. Often mixes live instruments with sampled or programmed elements.

indie pop.name: Indie Pop
indie pop.description: Independent, guitar-forward pop music with quirky production sensibilities and emotional sincerity. Born from the DIY ethos of the 1980s UK independent music scene. Jangly clean guitars, warm vintage synths, layered backing vocals, and relatable lyrical themes distinguish it from mainstream pop.
indie pop.production_notes: Often recorded in home or small studios. Mix is bright and wide. Guitar tone is clean to slightly overdriven with spring reverb. Vocals sit prominently. Production quality ranges from deliberately lo-fi to polished depending on the artist.

folk.name: Folk
folk.description: Acoustic, narrative-driven music with roots in oral storytelling traditions. From American Appalachian folk to British Isles ballads to contemporary indie folk. Defined by fingerpicked or strummed acoustic guitar, intimate vocals, and lyrical content that prioritizes story over production.
folk.production_notes: Minimal processing — room sound is embraced as part of the texture. Acoustic guitar is usually recorded with a ribbon or condenser mic with minimal compression. Vocals are warm, forward, and emotionally unguarded.

edm.name: Electronic Dance Music (EDM)
edm.description: Producer-driven electronic music engineered for maximum dancefloor and festival impact. Defined by the four-on-the-floor kick drum, aggressive sidechain compression, and tension-release drop structures. Encompasses house, trance, dubstep, and progressive electronic sub-genres.
edm.production_notes: Heavily compressed and limited for maximum loudness. Synthesizer layering is complex — often 10+ layers in the drop. Sidechain compression creates the characteristic pumping feel. Mixdowns optimized for club PA systems. BPM typically 128 for house, 140 for dubstep.

reggae.name: Reggae
reggae.description: Jamaican rhythm-based music emphasizing the offbeat. Developed in Kingston in the late 1960s, reggae is defined by the 'skank' — a short staccato guitar chord on the offbeat — combined with one-drop drumming and bass-forward mixing. Themes often include social consciousness and positivity.
reggae.production_notes: Bass sits prominently in the mix — often the loudest element. Guitar plays short staccato chords precisely on the 2nd and 4th sixteenth notes. Dub effects (spring reverb, tape delay) are common in reggae production and post-production remixing.

country.name: Country
country.description: American roots music blending Appalachian folk, blues, and Western cowboy traditions. Acoustic and electric guitars, pedal steel, fiddle, and honest storytelling about home, heartbreak, work, and American life. Modern country ranges from traditional acoustic to country-pop crossovers.
country.production_notes: Warm, dry recordings with natural room sound. Pedal steel is the single most distinctive timbral element. Vocals are prominent and emotionally direct — no heavy processing. Mix is open with plenty of headroom, unlike pop's wall-to-wall compression.

blues.name: Blues
blues.description: African American musical tradition expressing personal hardship and emotional truth through pentatonic scales, call-and-response vocal patterns, and expressive guitar work. The foundation of rock, jazz, and R&B. Defined by blue notes, string bends, and a direct emotional honesty that no other genre matches.
blues.production_notes: Raw, dry recording is the traditional blues aesthetic. Guitar is close-miked to capture fingertip noise and string buzz. Harmonica and slide guitar are defining timbral elements. Minimal reverb preserves intimacy and directness.

k-pop.name: K-Pop
k-pop.description: South Korean pop music defined by its maximalist production, precise group choreography, and genre-blending approach. K-pop packages EDM, R&B, hip-hop, and pop into highly polished releases backed by major entertainment companies. Every element — music, visual, performance — is engineered as a unified product.
k-pop.production_notes: Heavily produced and mastered for streaming loudness. Multiple vocal layers across 4-8 group members. Electronic production with live instrument flourishes. Meticulous arrangement with frequent section changes to match choreography transitions. BPM typically 128-145.
